reasoning:
The weekly remittance series reflects the Fed's 'deferred asset' - its accumulated operating losses. When the Fed operates at a loss, this series grows more negative. The long-term trend has been negative, but the rate of decrease has slowed significantly in late 2025/early 2026, reflecting the Fed nearing profitability (e.g., due to lower interest expenses on reserves). 

Short-term (March 8): The most recent daily momentum in late February has been negative (-51, -262 in successive weeks). The first week of March lacks the major mid-quarter Treasury coupon payments (like those on Feb 15) that occasionally cause the series to spike upwards. Therefore, the immediate week-over-week change is likely to remain negative, warranting a lower probability (0.32) of an increase by March 8.

Medium-term (March 31): Over a four-week period, uncertainty increases. There is a higher chance that structural regime shifts (a transition to sustained Fed profitability) or end-of-quarter effects begin to overpower the weekly noise. As the Fed draws closer to positive net income, longer time horizons introduce more variance, pulling our estimate closer to 0.5. I estimate a 0.42 probability that the balance will be higher by the end of March compared to the March 1 baseline.
